traces.response:
Pour tracker la fermeture de ta popin avec un objectif de type « Click tracking » dans Kameleoon, il faut choisir le bon sélecteur CSS. Voici comment faire avec ta structure HTML :

### 1. Si tu veux tracker le clic sur le bouton de fermeture :
Le bouton a la classe `.kml-geomap-close`, donc le sélecteur CSS à utiliser est :
```css
.kml-geomap-close
```

### 2. Si tu veux tracker le clic sur la popin entière :
La popin a l’ID `kml-geomap-modal`, donc le sélecteur CSS est :
```css
#kml-geomap-modal
```

### 3. Si tu veux tracker le clic sur l’overlay (si tu ajoutes un overlay autour de la popin) :
Si l’élément overlay a un ID ou une classe, par exemple :
```html
<div id="kml-geomap-overlay">
```
Le sélecteur serait :
```css
#kml-geomap-overlay
```
Mais dans ton code, l’élément overlay n’a pas de classe ou d’ID spécifique. Si tu veux tracker le clic sur tout l’overlay, il est conseillé d’ajouter un ID ou une classe à cet élément, par exemple :
```javascript
var overlay = document.createElement("div");
overlay.id = "kml-geomap-overlay";
```
Et d’utiliser :
```css
#kml-geomap-overlay
```

### 4. Si tu veux tracker le clic sur n’importe quel endroit de la popin (hors bouton) :
Utilise le sélecteur de la popin principale :  
```css
#kml-geomap-modal
```

### 5. Si tu veux tracker le clic sur un bouton spécifique à l’intérieur de la popin :
Par exemple, pour le bouton d’activation de la géolocalisation :
```css
.kml-geomap-btn
```

---

**Résumé** :  
- Pour la fermeture via le bouton croix : `.kml-geomap-close`
- Pour la fermeture via l’overlay (si tu ajoutes un overlay) : `#kml-geomap-overlay`
- Pour la fermeture via la popin entière : `#kml-geomap-modal`

Tu peux saisir ce sélecteur dans la configuration de l’objectif « Click tracking » dans Kameleoon.

Pour plus de détails sur la configuration, consulte la documentation officielle :  
[user-manual/assets/goals/create-a-goal#click-tracking](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/goals/create-a-goal#click-tracking)

N’hésite pas à préciser si tu veux tracker le clic sur un élément particulier ou sur toute la zone !

traces.prompt:
when should this test end? 

Current Date: 15/06/2026 08:00

User is currently looking at the EXPERIMENT with ID: 392339
traces.response:
The end of a Kameleoon experiment (test) can be determined in several ways, depending on how it was configured:

1. **Scheduled End Date**: If you set an end date when scheduling the experiment, the test will automatically pause at that date and time. You can check or modify this in the experiment's schedule settings. When the scheduled end date is reached, Kameleoon pauses the experiment, but you can resume it at any time.

2. **Statistical Confidence**: For A/B experiments, it is recommended not to set an end date before launch. Instead, use the confidence rate as your primary indicator for when to stop the experiment. The test should end when the reliability (confidence) rate stabilizes at your configured value, or when you have collected enough traffic to reach statistical significance.

3. **Traffic Threshold**: You can also configure the experiment to end automatically when a defined traffic threshold is reached.

4. **Manual Stop**: You can manually stop or pause the experiment at any time from the Kameleoon dashboard.

**Best Practice:** Always review the confidence rate before analyzing results or ending the experiment. For time-bound or event-based experiments, use a scheduled end date. For most A/B tests, wait for statistical significance.

For more details, see the official documentation:
- [Schedule an experiment](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/experimentation/web-experimentation/configure-and-launch/finalizing-an-experiment#schedule)

If you need to know the specific end condition for experiment ID 392339, you should check its schedule and configuration in your Kameleoon dashboard.

La variation « CTA » présente des hausses modérées sur les taux d’inscription (+4,74 %) et de confirmation (+5,16 %), mais avec des niveaux de fiabilité faibles (respectivement 34,28 % et 3,08 %). Les autres métriques, y compris les taux de login et de leads premium/family/duo, affichent des variations faibles ou contradictoires, toutes sans signification statistique (fiabilité < 95 %). Les intervalles de confiance larges indiquent une forte incertitude sur les effets réels.

Recommendations :  
Aucune variation ne démontre d’effet clair ou fiable. Il est recommandé de ne pas déployer ces changements en l’état. Une itération sur le design ou une augmentation de la taille d’échantillon pourrait être envisagée pour clarifier les tendances observées, mais à ce stade, les résultats sont neutres et non concluants.

traces.prompt:
J'aimerai créer un objectif pour une popin informative. Cette popin intervient quand l'utilisateur arrive sur une page. L'utilisateur prend l'information et il clique n'importe ou sur l'écran. la popin disparaît. Mais je ne sais pas quel objectif mettre pour répondre à ce use case

Current Date: 15/06/2026 07:59
traces.response:
Pour votre cas d'usage (popin informative qui disparaît au clic n'importe où sur l’écran), le type d’objectif le plus adapté dans Kameleoon est un objectif de type « Click tracking ». Cela permet de mesurer le nombre d’utilisateurs qui interagissent avec la popin (c’est-à-dire, qui la ferment en cliquant).

Voici comment procéder :

1. Créez un objectif de type « Click tracking ».
2. Sélectionnez l’élément HTML correspondant à la popin ou à son overlay (zone de fond qui ferme la popin au clic).
3. Si la popin se ferme au clic sur l’overlay ou sur la popin elle-même, ciblez le sélecteur CSS de cet élément lors de la configuration de l’objectif.

Points importants :
- Si la popin disparaît au clic sur l’overlay, ciblez l’élément overlay.
- Si elle disparaît au clic sur la popin elle-même ou sur un bouton spécifique, ciblez cet élément.
- Si le clic peut être n’importe où sur l’écran, il faut que le sélecteur corresponde à la zone qui déclenche la fermeture.

Pour configurer ce type d’objectif, suivez la documentation officielle :  
[Click tracking](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/goals/create-a-goal#click-tracking)

Résumé de la démarche :
- Dans Kameleoon, créez un nouvel objectif.
- Choisissez « Click tracking ».
- Indiquez l’URL de la page où la popin apparaît.
- Sélectionnez l’élément à tracker (overlay, popin, etc.).
- Validez et associez cet objectif à votre campagne.

Si la fermeture de la popin est gérée par un script personnalisé, vous pouvez aussi déclencher un objectif via l’API JavaScript de Kameleoon, par exemple avec :
```javascript
Kameleoon.API.Goals.processConversion(goalId);
```
(à placer dans la fonction qui ferme la popin).

Pour plus de détails sur la création d’un objectif de clic, consultez la documentation :  
/user-manual/assets/goals/create-a-goal

N’hésitez pas à préciser si la fermeture se fait sur un élément particulier ou sur tout le document, pour un conseil plus ciblé !

in a second paragraph starting with "Recommendations:".

Leverage your statistical knowledge to analyse each piece of information
before crafting your final output.

If the experiment seems valid — meaning that the setup and data collection worked as intended
(e.g., traffic flowed, metrics were measured, no issues spotted) — then the recommendation can be:
  1. Ship for success if a key metric improves without regressions;
  2. Abort and iterate if regressions are detected and metrics worsen;
  3. Iterate, abandon or ship if it's neutral but informative — when no effect is detected
     but the test was strong enough to detect one if it existed.

traces.prompt:
We have not set an end date, but Kamaleoon can predict it, right?

Current Date: 15/06/2026 07:56
traces.response:
Yes, Kameleoon can estimate the anticipated end date (duration) of your test, even if you have not set a fixed end date.

On the experiment finalization page, you will find an option to "Estimate the experiment duration." Here’s how it works:

- You provide:
  - Average daily visitors to the tested pages
  - Current conversion rate for your main goal
  - Minimum Detectable Effect (MDE) you want to identify
  - Desired reliability rate (confidence level, usually 95%)

Kameleoon will then estimate how long your experiment should run to reach statistically significant results, based on your inputs and the experiment setup (traffic allocation, number of variations, etc.).

This is an estimate. After launch, you should monitor the reliability index on the results page for actual progress.

For more details, see:  
https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/experimentation/web-experimentation/configure-and-launch/finalizing-an-experiment#estimate-an-experiment-s-duration

To see the anticipated end date of your test in Kameleoon:

1. Go to your experiment's configuration page.

For more details, see the documentation:

traces.prompt:
Wie sollte ich im Kameleoon Insights Test Ideas die Seiten am besten strukturieren? Eher Pagetypes zuzsammenfassen wie PDPs? Oder sollen produktspezifische Seiten wie PDP und PLP in eine Ideate Gruppe zusammengefasst werden? Wie ist hier die Empfehlung?

Current Date: 15/06/2026 07:23
traces.response:
Die Kameleoon-Dokumentation gibt keine explizite Empfehlung, wie du Seiten im Bereich Insights > Test Ideas (PBX Ideate) am besten strukturieren solltest – also ob du Pagetypes wie PDPs (Product Detail Pages) zusammenfassen oder produktspezifische Seiten wie PDP und PLP (Product Listing Pages) in eine gemeinsame Ideate-Gruppe aufnehmen solltest.

Allerdings gibt es folgende Hinweise und Best Practices:

- Du kannst im Tab „Scanned pages“ gezielt einzelne URLs hinzufügen, um Testideen für diese Seiten zu generieren. Das spricht dafür, dass du sowohl einzelne Seiten als auch Seitentypen (z. B. alle PDPs) scannen lassen kannst, je nachdem, wie granular du deine Hypothesen und Testideen erhalten möchtest.
- PBX Ideate analysiert jede gescannte Seite individuell und generiert darauf basierende Optimierungshypothesen. Die Empfehlungen und Ideen sind also immer auf die jeweilige Seite bzw. den jeweiligen Seitentyp zugeschnitten.
- Für Multi-Page-Experimente kannst du nach dem Erstellen einer Variation auf einer Produktseite zur nächsten wechseln und die AI-generierten Änderungen erneut anwenden. So kannst du sicherstellen, dass die Tests konsistent auf mehreren ähnlichen Seiten laufen.

**Empfehlung aus der Praxis:**
- Wenn du Hypothesen und Testideen für einen bestimmten Seitentyp (z. B. alle PDPs) erhalten möchtest, empfiehlt es sich, exemplarisch eine oder mehrere typische PDPs zu scannen und die Ideen dann auf alle PDPs zu übertragen.
- Wenn du sehr spezifische Hypothesen für einzelne Produkte oder Seiten brauchst, solltest du diese Seiten einzeln scannen.
- Die Gruppierung in Ideate-Gruppen ist flexibel: Du kannst PDP und PLP in eine Gruppe aufnehmen, wenn du übergreifende Ideen für den gesamten Kaufprozess suchst. Für gezielte Optimierungen empfiehlt sich aber meist eine Trennung nach Seitentypen, da sich die Nutzerintention und die Optimierungshebel (Levers) zwischen PDP und PLP unterscheiden.

**Fazit:**  
- Für strukturierte und relevante Testideen empfiehlt sich meist eine Gruppierung nach Seitentypen (z. B. PDP, PLP, Startseite, Warenkorb).
- Für übergreifende Hypothesen kannst du auch mehrere Seitentypen in eine Ideate-Gruppe aufnehmen, solltest dann aber die Unterschiede bei der Auswertung beachten.

Weitere Details findest du in der Dokumentation:
- [Scanned pages – Testideen generieren](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/experimentation/web-experimentation/prompt-based-experiments/pbx-ideate/generate-test-ideas)
- [FAQ zu PBX Ideate](https://do…

traces.prompt:
a t'on moyen de voir dans le back office si un client a bien mis en place l'itp manager?

Current Date: 15/06/2026 07:05
traces.response:
La documentation Kameleoon ne mentionne pas la possibilité de vérifier directement dans le back office si un client a bien mis en place l’ITP Manager (c’est-à-dire la synchronisation du cookie kameleoonVisitorCode côté serveur pour contourner les restrictions ITP de Safari).

Voici ce que dit la documentation officielle :
- La mise en place de l’ITP Manager consiste à configurer le serveur ou le CDN pour générer et ajouter le cookie kameleoonVisitorCode via une entête HTTP, ou à implémenter un code spécifique côté backend. Plus d’informations ici : https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/web-experimentation/technical-concepts/itp-management
- Il n’est pas indiqué qu’un indicateur ou une alerte existe dans le back office pour confirmer la bonne installation de cette solution.

En résumé : il n’existe pas de vérification automatique ou de statut visible dans le back office Kameleoon pour savoir si l’ITP Manager est bien en place. La vérification doit donc se faire côté technique, par exemple en contrôlant la présence du cookie kameleoonVisitorCode dans les entêtes HTTP de réponse serveur sur Safari.

Pour plus de détails sur la mise en place et la vérification technique :  
https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/web-experimentation/technical-concepts/itp-management

traces.prompt:
What I want to do is make kameleoon read the new custom data values when they are updated and apply the according context in real time

Current Date: 14/06/2026 20:33
traces.response:
Great clarification! Here’s how Kameleoon handles custom data updates and context evaluation in real time, and what you can do to ensure your personalisation reacts immediately to changes:

---

## How Kameleoon Handles Custom Data Updates

- **Custom Data and Targeting:**  
  When you update a custom data value using `Kameleoon.API.Data.setCustomData("key", value)`, Kameleoon will update the value for the current visitor.
- **Context/Segment Evaluation:**  
  Kameleoon’s targeting engine evaluates segments and contexts when the page loads and when custom data is updated via the API.
- **Real-Time Application:**  
  If your personalisation or experiment is set up to trigger based on custom data, and you update that data using the API, Kameleoon should automatically re-evaluate targeting and apply the correct context **in real time**.

---

## How to Force Kameleoon to Re-Evaluate Contexts

If you are updating custom data and want Kameleoon to immediately re-check targeting and apply the correct context (e.g., show/hide a widget or switch personalisation):

1. **Update the Custom Data:**
   ```js
   Kameleoon.API.Data.setCustomData("yourCustomDataKey", newValue);
   ```
2. **Kameleoon Should React Automatically:**  
   In most cases, Kameleoon will automatically re-evaluate targeting and apply the correct context as soon as the custom data is updated.

3. **If It Does Not React Automatically:**  
   - Make sure your custom data is used directly in the segment/context targeting.
   - If you are using custom triggers or advanced logic, ensure your code or triggers listen for changes and call the necessary Kameleoon APIs.

---

## Troubleshooting: If Context Does Not Update in Real Time

- **Check for Asynchronous Custom Data:**  
  If your custom data is set asynchronously (e.g., after an API call), ensure you call `setCustomData` only after the value is available.
- **Check Segment/Context Setup:**  
  Make sure your segment or context is set up to use the custom data as a targeting condition.
- **No Page Reload Needed:**  
  You should not need to reload the page; Kameleoon is designed to react to custom data changes in real time.

---

## Example: Real-Time Update

```js
// User action changes a flag
Kameleoon.API.Data.setCustomData("isLoggedIn", true);
// Kameleoon will now re-evaluate all contexts/segments using "isLoggedIn"
```

If your widget or personalisation is not updating, check:
- The custom data key matches exactly in your segment/context.
- The…

traces.prompt:
This didn't work as expected - window.Kameleoon.API.Core.processNewVisitorData() - what is the correct method/

Current Date: 14/06/2026 20:31
traces.response:
The method `window.Kameleoon.API.Core.processNewVisitorData()` is **not documented** as a public or recommended way to reset or refresh visitor data in Kameleoon.

**The correct and supported way to reset a custom data value** for the current visitor is:

```js
Kameleoon.API.Data.resetCustomData("yourCustomDataKey");
```
This will remove the value for that custom data key for the current visitor.

---

**If you want to simulate a new visitor (i.e., reset all visitor-level data):**
- There is no public JavaScript API documented by Kameleoon to fully "reset" the visitor (i.e., generate a new visitor code and clear all visitor-scoped data) from the browser console.
- The only supported way is to clear your browser's cookies and local storage for your site, then reload the page. This will generate a new visitor code and reset all visitor-scoped data.

---

**Summary:**
- Use `Kameleoon.API.Data.resetCustomData("yourCustomDataKey")` to reset individual custom data values.
- To fully reset the visitor, clear cookies and local storage for your site.

**References:**
- [resetCustomData API documentation](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/apis/activation-api-js/api-reference/api-reference#resetcustomdata)

If you need to reset all custom data or simulate a new visitor, let me know and I can provide step-by-step instructions!

**Alternative ways to check custom data values:**

### 1. Using the Kameleoon API v1 (most common in older projects)
You can check the value of custom data by inspecting the following objects in your browser console:

- For **visit-scoped** or **page-scoped** custom data:
  ```js
  Kameleoon.API.CurrentVisit.customData
  ```
- For **visitor-scoped** custom data:
  ```js
  Kameleoon.API.Visitor.customData
  ```

These will return an object with all custom data and their values. You can then access an individual value like this:
```js
Kameleoon.API.CurrentVisit.customData["yourCustomDataKey"]
```
or
```js
Kameleoon.API.Visitor.customData["yourCustomDataKey"]
```

### 2. If you use custom JavaScript code for custom data
If your custom data is set via custom JavaScript, you may need to check the variable or storage location (e.g., `window`, `localStorage`, or a data layer) where your code stores the value.

---

**Summary:**  
- Try `Kameleoon.API.CurrentVisit.customData["yourCustomDataKey"]` or `Kameleoon.API.Visitor.customData["yourCustomDataKey"]` in your console.
- If you still can't find your value, check how your custom data is defined in the Kameleoon app (scope and acquisition method).

traces.prompt:
My campaign is a personalisation that uses the new personalisation builder. I have several contexts and targeting rules. The targeting rule of each context has a different segment which are based on custom data. These flags are all set to false by default so no segment is matched to start with, but the flags change as the user interacts with the site. Each targeting rule also includes a trigger which checks the presence of an element on the page.

Current Date: 14/06/2026 19:16
traces.response:
Based on your setup and the Kameleoon documentation, here’s why your simulation widget does not open when no segment is matched, and how you can test your personalisation:

---

### Why the Widget Does Not Open

- **No Segment Matched by Default:**  
  Since all your custom data flags are set to `false` by default, **no segment is matched** when the simulation starts.  
  In Kameleoon, if a visitor does not match any segment (i.e., does not meet the targeting conditions), the personalisation (and thus the widget) will not be triggered or displayed.

- **Triggers and Custom Data:**  
  Each context’s targeting rule requires both a segment (based on custom data) and a trigger (element presence).  
  If neither the segment nor the trigger is matched at simulation start, the personalisation will not activate.

---

### How to Simulate and Test Your Personalisation

To see your widget in simulation, you need to ensure that the simulation visitor matches at least one segment and trigger. Here’s how:

#### 1. **Set Custom Data Values in Simulation**
- Use the browser console to set the required custom data values to `true` (or the value that matches your segment condition).
- Example (replace `customDataKey` and `value` with your actual keys/values):
  ```js
  Kameleoon.API.Data.setCustomData("customDataKey", true);
  ```
- Refresh the simulation page after setting the custom data.

#### 2. **Ensure the Trigger Condition is Met**
- If your trigger checks for the presence of a DOM element, make sure that element exists on the page when simulating.
- You can manually add the element in the browser console if needed:
  ```js
  var el = document.createElement('div');
  el.className = 'your-element-class';
  document.body.appendChild(el);
  ```

#### 3. **Force Variation Display (if available)**
- In the simulation panel, use the “Force display” or “Variation selector” to override targeting and display the widget, if this option is available.

---

### Documentation References

- [Targeting condition for segments](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/custom-data#targeting-condition-for-segments)
- [Technical segment builder options](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/segments/create-a-segment#technical)

Based on your description and the Kameleoon documentation, here’s what’s happening and how you can troubleshoot:

### Situation Recap
- You are using simulation parameters like `#kameleoon-experiment-id=233892&kameleoon-simulation=true&kameleoon-language=en`.
- The simulation widget **does not open** for experiment ID `233892`, but **does open** for other experiment/campaign IDs.

### What This Means
This strongly suggests the issue is **specific to the configuration or status of experiment 233892**, not a general simulation or widget problem.

### Key Troubleshooting Steps

1. **Check if the Campaign is Active and Online**
   - The simulation will not work if the experiment is not active or not published.
   - Use the Kameleoon dashboard to confirm that experiment 233892 is online.

2. **Check Targeting and Segments**
   - If the simulation widget does not open, it may be because the current visitor/session does **not meet the targeting conditions** for this experiment.
   - In simulation mode, you can usually force the display of a variation, but if the campaign is misconfigured or has restrictive targeting, it may not show.
   - Review the targeting criteria and segments for experiment 233892.

3. **Check for Errors in the Console**
   - Open your browser’s developer console and look for any JavaScript errors or Kameleoon-specific errors when trying to simulate experiment 233892.

4. **Compare with Working Campaigns**
   - Since other campaigns work, compare the configuration (targeting, triggers, status, widget association) of experiment 233892 with a campaign that works in simulation.

5. **Widget Association**
   - Ensure that the widget is correctly associated with experiment 233892. If the widget is not linked or is inactive for this campaign, it will not display.

6. **Variation Assignment**
   - In simulation, you can usually select which variation to preview. If you are assigned to the reference/original variation (which may not display the widget), try forcing a different variation.

7. **Check for Legacy or Obsolete Widgets**
   - If experiment 233892 uses an old or deprecated widget, it may not be compatible with the current simulation tools.

traces.prompt:
The simulation widget doesn't open when I try to simulate my personalisation.

Current Date: 14/06/2026 19:04
traces.response:
The Kameleoon documentation does not provide a dedicated troubleshooting section specifically for the simulation widget in personalizations, but based on the official guidance for similar issues (such as the Graphic Editor not loading), here are recommended steps you can take if the simulation widget does not open:

### Troubleshooting Steps

1. **Verify Kameleoon Script Installation**
   - Ensure the Kameleoon script is correctly installed on the page you are trying to simulate. Use your browser’s developer tools to check for the presence of the script.

2. **Check Domain Configuration**
   - Make sure the domain and all relevant subdomains are correctly configured in your Kameleoon project settings.

3. **Clear Cache**
   - Try emptying your browser cache or using a private/incognito window to rule out cached files interfering with the simulation.

4. **Allow Third-Party Cookies**
   - Ensure your browser allows third-party cookies, as these are often required for Kameleoon tools to function.

5. **Disable Ad Blockers and Extensions**
   - Ad blockers or privacy extensions (like Ghostery or uBlock Origin) can prevent the simulation widget from loading. Disable these and try again.

6. **Disable VPN**
   - If you are using a VPN, try disconnecting it, as it may interfere with loading Kameleoon resources.

7. **Try a Different Network or Browser**
   - Network restrictions or browser-specific issues can sometimes block the widget. Try switching networks or using a different browser (Chrome Dev is recommended for best compatibility).

8. **Check for CORS Policy Errors**
   - Look for CORS errors in your browser’s console. If present, your server may need to whitelist Kameleoon domains.

### Additional Notes

- The simulation will only work on the URL you entered when creating your widget. Double-check that you are simulating on the correct URL.
- If your widget uses custom CSS or JS, ensure there are no errors in your code that could prevent rendering.

For more details, see:
- [Simulate your widgets](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/simulate-your-widgets)
- [Troubleshooting the Graphic editor](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/experimentation/web-experimentation/graphic-based-experiments/troubleshooting-the-graphic-editor)

If you have followed all these steps and the simulation widget still does not open, there may be a technical issue requiring support from Kameleoon’s technical team.

traces.prompt:
Write a summary of the results of this experiment based on the information provided below
in a first paragraph starting with "Insights:".

Be concise and stay within 100 words.
Do not mention the name or details of the experiment itself.
Focus only on the improvement or decline of metrics and the reliability of their estimates.

Provide clear, actionable recommendations based on the trends observed
in a second paragraph starting with "Recommendations:".

Leverage your statistical knowledge to analyse each piece of information
before crafting your final output.

If the experiment seems valid — meaning that the setup and data collection worked as intended
(e.g., traffic flowed, metrics were measured, no issues spotted) — then the recommendation can be:
  1. Ship for success if a key metric improves without regressions;
  2. Abort and iterate if regressions are detected and metrics worsen;
  3. Iterate, abandon or ship if it's neutral but informative — when no effect is detected
     but the test was strong enough to detect one if it existed.

Answer in en.
Use only the following results:
Experiment Metadata:

Name: Experiment 4 - 50%

Variations:
- Original
- v1

Sample Sizes:
- Original: 243013 users
- v1: 242777 users

Unit Type: users

Analysis level: Conversion rate

Randomization Method: MAB

Statistical Method Used:
- Inference Approach: Sequential testing
- Test Type: Two-tailed
- Confidence Level Required: 95%
- Adjustment for Multiple Comparisons: None


Metric Name: c…
traces.response:
Insights:  
Across all measured metrics, differences between the Original and v1 variants were minimal and fell well within wide confidence intervals, with no metric reaching statistical significance (reliability < 1%). Conversion rates for key actions such as pageviews, listing clicks, email leads, phone leads, listing saves, and email lead forms showed negligible lifts or declines, all with high uncertainty and overlapping intervals.

Recommendations:  
Given the absence of statistically significant effects and the high uncertainty in all estimates, there is no evidence to support a meaningful impact of v1. It is recommended to iterate or abandon this variant, as the test was sufficiently powered to detect an effect if one existed.

traces.prompt:
Where can I edit the global script for each project?

Current Date: 14/06/2026 12:39
traces.response:
You can edit the global script for each project in the Kameleoon platform by following these steps:

1. Go to the Kameleoon app and click on the **Admin** menu.
2. Navigate to **Settings** > **Configuration** > **General**.
3. Scroll down to find the **Global script** section.

The global script is a JavaScript snippet that runs on every page where the Kameleoon tag is loaded, before all experiments, personalizations, segments, and triggers. It is used to define variables, helper functions, or tracking code that should be available across all experiments in the project.

For more details and best practices, see:
- /user-manual/project-management/manage-your-projects#general
- /developer-docs/web-experimentation/best-practices/code-debt-cleanup#5-global-script

After editing, click **Validate** to apply your changes.

I've set the page target to all pages. The personalisation still isn't appearing as a campaign on the page.

If your personalization is set to "Online," targeted to all pages, and you are visiting from the correct IP address but it still does not appear as a campaign on the page or in the Debug Assistant, here are the most likely causes and next troubleshooting steps:

---

## 1. **Segment Association**
- Make sure your segment (with the IP address condition) is actually associated with the personalization.
- In the personalization's **Targeting** step, you must explicitly select your segment.  
  See: [Set up a segment in a personalization](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/segments/set-up-a-segment-in-a-campaign#set-up-a-segment-in-a-personalization)

## 2. **Widget or Content Association**
- If your personalization is supposed to display a widget or content, ensure a widget is associated and active in the personalization.
- See: [Associate a widget with a personalization](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/use-widgets-in-your-campaigns/associate-a-widget-with-a-personalization)

## 3. **Prioritization and Conflicts**
- If you have other personalizations running, check the prioritization settings. A higher-priority personalization may be taking precedence and preventing yours from displaying.
- See: [Prioritization](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/web-personalization/set-up-and-launch-a-personalization#prioritization)

## 4. **Propagation Delay**
- After making changes, it can take a few minutes for updates to propagate to your site. Wait a few minutes and refresh the page.

## 5. **Debug Assistant and Script Loading**
- Confirm the Kameleoon script is loaded on your page (check browser network tab for requests to `kameleoon.com` or `kameleoon.io`).
- Make sure you are using the latest version of the Debug Assistant.

## 6. **No Other Targeting Conditions**
- Double-check that there are no other targeting conditions (such as device, browser, or custom data) that could prevent you from being targeted.

---

### Step-by-Step Checklist

1. **Personalization is Online**  
2. **Segment with IP Address is Created**  
3. **Segment is Associated with the Personalization**  
4. **Personalization is Targeting All Pages**  
5. **Widget or Content is Associated (if applicable)**  
6. **No Higher-Priority Personalizations are Blocking**  
7. **You are Visiting from the Correct IP Address**  
8. **No Ad Blockers or Extensions are Interfering**  
9. **Kameleoon Script is Loaded on the Page**  
10. **Waited a Few Minutes for Propagation** …

traces.prompt:
The personalisation still isn't listed in the debug assistant

Current Date: 13/06/2026 20:00
traces.response:
If your personalization is set to "Online," targeted to your IP address, and you are visiting from that IP but it still does not appear in the Debug Assistant, here are the most likely causes and troubleshooting steps based on Kameleoon documentation:

---

## 1. **Check Personalization Status**
- Double-check in the Kameleoon dashboard that the personalization status is "Online" (not Draft, Paused, or Stopped).
- Only "Online" personalizations are executed and visible in the Debug Assistant.

## 2. **Confirm Segment Condition**
- Ensure your segment uses the **Visitor IP Address** condition (not a custom data or ID field).
- The value must exactly match your current public IP address. Check your IP at https://whatismyipaddress.com/ and compare it to the segment value.

## 3. **Clear Cache and Cookies**
- Sometimes, browser cache or cookies can prevent the latest segment or personalization changes from being recognized. Try clearing your cache or using a private/incognito window.

## 4. **Reload the Page**
- After setting the personalization online and updating the segment, reload your website page and then open the Debug Assistant.

## 5. **Check for Ad Blockers or Extensions**
- Ad blockers or privacy extensions can block Kameleoon scripts or requests, preventing personalizations from running. Disable these extensions or whitelist your site and Kameleoon domains.

## 6. **Check Site Code and Environment**
- Make sure you are testing on the correct environment (production, staging, etc.) and that the Kameleoon script is installed and running on the page you are testing.

## 7. **Wait for Propagation**
- Sometimes, changes to segments or personalizations can take a few minutes to propagate. Wait a few minutes and try again.

## 8. **Debug Assistant Version**
- Ensure you are using the latest version of the Kameleoon Debug Assistant Chrome extension.

---

### Additional Documentation References
- [Manage personalizations](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/web-personalization/manage-personalizations#status)
- [Create a segment](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/segments/create-a-segment#visitor-characteristics)
- [Debug Assistant: Personalizations tab](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/developer-tools/kameleoon-debug-assistant-extension#personalizations)
- [Ad blockers and Kameleoon](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/faq#how-do-ad-blockers-affect-kameleoon)

---

### If It Still Doesn't Work
If you have checked all of the above…

traces.prompt:
I've created a segment based on ID and set the value to my IP - "77.103.44.184"

Current Date: 13/06/2026 19:58
traces.response:
You are on the right track! Kameleoon allows you to create a segment based on the visitor's IP address, which is a common and effective way to restrict a personalization to yourself for safe testing.

**Important details from the documentation:**
- The correct targeting condition is **Visitor IP Address**.
- You should use the "is" or "is among" operator and enter your IP address (e.g., `77.103.44.184`).
- This ensures only visitors from that IP address (you) will be targeted by the personalization.

**Reference:**  
See the "Visitor characteristics" section in the segment creation documentation:  
https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/segments/create-a-segment#visitor-characteristics

**Checklist for your setup:**
1. In your segment, confirm you used the "Visitor IP Address" condition.
2. Make sure the value matches your current public IP address exactly.
3. Set your personalization to "Online."
4. Visit your site from the IP address you specified.
5. Use the Debug Assistant to verify that you are targeted and the personalization is running.

**Note:**  
If your IP address changes (for example, if you are on a dynamic IP or using a VPN), you will need to update the segment with your new IP.

If you followed these steps, your personalization will only be visible to you and not to other users. If you still do not see the personalization, double-check your IP address (you can use a service like https://whatismyipaddress.com/) and ensure there are no typos in the segment condition.

Let me know if you need step-by-step instructions or if you encounter any issues!

traces.prompt:
But if I set it live, won't users be exposed to it/

Current Date: 13/06/2026 19:47
traces.response:
Great question! By default, setting a personalization to "Online" does make it eligible to be triggered for real users according to its targeting and scheduling rules. However, there are safe ways to test your personalization without exposing it to your actual users:

---

## Safe Ways to Test an "Online" Personalization

### 1. **Restrict Targeting to Yourself**
- Set the targeting conditions so that only you (or a test segment) can see the personalization.  
  For example, target by a specific IP address, a unique cookie, or a test user segment.
- This way, even though the personalization is "Online," only you will be exposed to it.

### 2. **Use Scheduling**
- Set the schedule so the personalization is only active during a specific time window when you are testing, or set it to start in the future and test before the window opens.

### 3. **Use Capping**
- Set visitor capping to 1 or a very low number, so only the first visitor (you) will see it.

### 4. **QA/Preproduction Environment**
- If possible, test on a staging or QA environment that mirrors production, but is not accessible to real users.

### 5. **Simulation Mode**
- The simulation widget is designed for previewing, but as you noticed, it only works for "Online" personalizations. With restricted targeting, you can safely use simulation and the Debug Assistant.

---

## Documentation References
- [Manage personalizations](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/web-personalization/manage-personalizations#status)
- [Set up and launch a personalization](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/web-personalization/set-up-and-launch-a-personalization)
- [Debug Assistant: Personalizations tab](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/developer-tools/kameleoon-debug-assistant-extension#personalizations)

---

**Summary:**  
You can safely set your personalization to "Online" for testing by restricting its targeting to yourself or a test group. This ensures real users are not exposed while you debug and preview the experience.

If you need help setting up a test segment or targeting rule, let me know your use case and I can provide step-by-step instructions!

traces.prompt:
The personalisation isn't listed in debug assistant. Does it need to be online?

Current Date: 13/06/2026 19:46
traces.response:
Yes, your personalization must be set to "Online" for it to appear and be testable in the Kameleoon Debug Assistant.

**Key points from the documentation:**
- The Debug Assistant only lists personalizations that are running (i.e., online and active) on the page you are testing.
- If your personalization is in "Draft," "Paused," or "Stopped" status, it will not be listed in the Debug Assistant and will not be executed on your site.
- You can manage and change the status of your personalizations from the Personalizations Dashboard. Only "Online" personalizations are executed and visible for debugging and simulation.

traces.prompt:
My personalisation isn't opening in the simulation widget

Current Date: 13/06/2026 19:43
traces.response:
If your personalization isn't opening in the simulation widget, here are the most relevant troubleshooting steps and explanations based on the Kameleoon documentation:

### 1. Simulation Mode May Force Non-Exposition
When using the simulation widget, Kameleoon may intentionally prevent the personalization from displaying. In simulation mode, if the personalization was triggered but not displayed, the "Not Exposed Reason" field will indicate `SIMULATION`. This is expected behavior in simulation and does not occur in production. If the personalization was triggered on the current page and the "active" property is false, the reason will be available; otherwise, it will be null.

**Reference:**  
See the "Personalizations" section in the Debug Assistant documentation:  
https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/developer-tools/kameleoon-debug-assistant-extension#personalizations

### 2. Other Common Reasons for Non-Display
The "Not Exposed Reason" field in the Debug Assistant can also show other reasons why a personalization is not displayed, such as:
- **GLOBAL_EXCLUSION**: Visitor is globally excluded.
- **PERSONALIZATION_EXCLUSION**: Visitor is excluded from this personalization (e.g., in the control group).
- **PRIORITY**: Another personalization has higher priority.
- **SCHEDULE**: Personalization is off according to its schedule.
- **PERSONALIZATION_CAPPING**: Global capping reached.
- **VISITOR_CAPPING**: Visitor-specific capping reached.
- **SCENARIO**: Scenario conditions not fulfilled.

### 3. How to Troubleshoot
- Use the [Kameleoon Debug Assistant Chrome extension](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/developer-tools/kameleoon-debug-assistant-extension) to inspect which personalizations are running, their status, and the "Not Exposed Reason".
- Check your simulation URL and targeting conditions.
- Ensure your widget is correctly associated with the personalization and is active.

### 4. Simulation URL
You can change the default preview URL for your simulation in the general settings of your personalization.  
See: https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/web-personalization/set-up-and-launch-a-personalization#step-4-configure-general-settings-optional

---

**Summary:**  
If your personalization isn't opening in the simulation widget, check the "Not Exposed Reason" in the Debug Assistant. In simulation mode, non-exposition is often intentional and indicated by the `SIMULATION` reason. For other issues, review targeting, scheduling, and capping s…

traces.prompt:
Write a summary of the results of this experiment based on the information provided below
in a first paragraph starting with "Insights:".

Be concise and stay within 100 words.
Do not mention the name or details of the experiment itself.
Focus only on the improvement or decline of metrics and the reliability of their estimates.

Provide clear, actionable recommendations based on the trends observed
in a second paragraph starting with "Recommendations:".

Leverage your statistical knowledge to analyse each piece of information
before crafting your final output.

If the experiment seems valid — meaning that the setup and data collection worked as intended
(e.g., traffic flowed, metrics were measured, no issues spotted) — then the recommendation can be:
  1. Ship for success if a key metric improves without regressions;
  2. Abort and iterate if regressions are detected and metrics worsen;
  3. Iterate, abandon or ship if it's neutral but informative — when no effect is detected
     but the test was strong enough to detect one if it existed.

Further testing with larger samples or refined hypotheses may be warranted.

Provide clear, actionable recommendations based on the trends observed
in a second paragraph starting with "Recommendations:".

Leverage your statistical knowledge to analyse each piece of information
before crafting your final output.

If the experiment seems valid — meaning that the setup and data collection worked as intended
(e.g., traffic flowed, metrics were measured, no issues spotted) — then the recommendation can be:
  1. Ship for success if a key metric improves without regressions;
  2. Abort and iterate if regressions are detected and metrics worsen;
  3. Iterate, abandon or ship if it's neutral but informative — when no effect is detected
     but the test was strong enough to detect one if it existed.

traces.prompt:
Analyze the data of the Survey variation

Current Date: 12/06/2026 21:10

User is currently looking at the EXPERIMENT with ID: 375375
traces.response:
To analyze the data of a Survey variation in your Kameleoon experiment, here are the key steps and documentation references:

1. **Survey Data Export**:
   - You can export survey responses collected via your widget using the Widget Studio. The export is available in CSV format, which allows for detailed analysis in tools like Excel or Google Sheets.
   - See: [Survey Data Export](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/create-csat-nps-and-surveys/survey-data-export)

2. **Survey Elements**:
   - Survey widgets can include various elements (e.g., short/long answer, multiple choice, NPS, scale). Each element’s data will be included in the export, allowing you to analyze responses per question.
   - See: [Survey Elements](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/create-csat-nps-and-surveys/add-a-survey-element-to-your-widget)

3. **Exporting Survey Responses via API**:
   - You can programmatically export survey responses using the REST endpoint:  
     `GET /survey/{siteId}/{formId}/csv`
   - This will generate a CSV file with all responses for the specified survey.
   - See: [Export Survey Responses API](https://docs.kameleoon.com/api-reference/widgetstudio/export-survey-responses)

4. **Analyzing Results in Kameleoon**:
   - In the experiment analytics, you can view reliability rates, conversion data, and other metrics for each variation, including those with surveys.
   - You can adjust settings to show data per visit or visitor, and compare variations.
   - See: [Analyze Results - Overview](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/experiment-analytics/analyze-results/overview-menu) and [Results Page Settings](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/experiment-analytics/analyze-results/results-page-settings)

5. **Sending Survey Data to Google Sheets**:
   - For ongoing analysis, you can configure your survey to send responses directly to a Google Sheet, enabling real-time data review and custom reporting.
   - See: [Send Survey Responses to Google Sheets](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/create-csat-nps-and-surveys/send-survey-responses-to-google-sheets)

If you need step-by-step instructions for exporting or analyzing your survey data, let me know which method you prefer (CSV export, API, or Google Sheets), and I can provide more detailed guidance.

traces.prompt:
which will have the most impact current we have no conversions

Current Date: 12/06/2026 20:41
traces.response:
If your landing page currently has **no conversions at all**, the most impactful changes are usually those that address the most fundamental barriers to conversion. Here’s a prioritized approach, based on best practices and experimentation principles:

---

## 1. **Check for Technical Issues First**
- **Why:** Sometimes, zero conversions are due to broken forms, buttons, or tracking issues.
- **Action:**  
  - Test your form or CTA on multiple devices and browsers.
  - Ensure your analytics and conversion tracking are set up correctly.
- **Kameleoon Tip:** Use Kameleoon’s QA and preview features to verify experiment setup and tracking.  
  [QA and preview in Kameleoon](/guides/qa-preview)

---

## 2. **Simplify and Highlight Your Call-to-Action (CTA)**
- **Why:** If visitors don’t know what to do, or the CTA is unclear, they won’t convert.
- **Action:**  
  - Make your CTA button large, clear, and above the fold.
  - Use action-oriented text (“Get Started Free,” “Book a Call”).
  - Remove distractions around the CTA.
- **Kameleoon Tip:** Run an A/B test with a more prominent CTA.  
  [A/B testing basics](/ab-testing)

---

## 3. **Clarify Your Value Proposition**
- **Why:** If visitors don’t immediately understand what you offer and why it matters, they’ll leave.
- **Action:**  
  - Rewrite your headline and subheadline to clearly state the benefit.
  - Add a short supporting paragraph or bullet points.
- **Kameleoon Tip:** Test different headlines and value propositions.

---

## 4. **Reduce Friction in Forms**
- **Why:** Long or complex forms are a major barrier.
- **Action:**  
  - Only ask for essential information (e.g., just email or phone).
  - Remove unnecessary fields.
- **Kameleoon Tip:** Test a single-field form vs. your current form.

---

## 5. **Add Trust Signals**
- **Why:** New visitors need to trust you before converting.
- **Action:**  
  - Add testimonials, reviews, or trust badges (even if just “Secure checkout” or “Trusted by X customers”).
- **Kameleoon Tip:** Test the impact of adding social proof.

---

## 6. **Check for Mobile Usability**
- **Why:** Many visitors are on mobile, and a poor mobile experience kills conversions.
- **Action:**  
  - Test your page on mobile devices.
  - Ensure forms and CTAs are easy to use on small screens.

---

## 7. **Remove Distractions**
- **Why:** Too many links, popups, or navigation options can distract from your main goal.
- **Action:**  
  - Remove unnecessary links and focus the page on your…

traces.prompt:
do you have some ideas to increase conversion on a small business landing page

Current Date: 12/06/2026 20:41
traces.response:
Absolutely! Increasing conversion on a small business landing page is a common goal, and Kameleoon provides a range of tools and best practices to help you achieve this through experimentation and personalization. Here are some actionable ideas, grounded in experimentation principles and Kameleoon’s capabilities:

---

### 1. **A/B Test Your Value Proposition**
- **What to do:** Test different headlines, subheadings, and value propositions to see which resonates most with your audience.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Use Kameleoon’s A/B testing feature to easily set up and analyze these tests.  
  [Learn more about A/B testing with Kameleoon](/ab-testing)

---

### 2. **Optimize Call-to-Action (CTA) Buttons**
- **What to do:** Experiment with different CTA texts (“Get Started,” “Try Free,” “Book a Demo”), colors, and placements.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Use visual editor or code-based experiments to modify CTAs and track conversion rates.

---

### 3. **Personalize Content Based on Visitor Segments**
- **What to do:** Show different content or offers to new vs. returning visitors, or based on location/device.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Use Kameleoon’s segmentation and personalization features to target specific user groups.  
  [Read about segmentation and targeting](/guides/segmentation)

---

### 4. **Reduce Form Fields**
- **What to do:** Test shorter vs. longer forms to see if reducing friction increases sign-ups or leads.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Run experiments to compare conversion rates for different form lengths.

---

### 5. **Add Social Proof**
- **What to do:** Test the impact of adding testimonials, reviews, or trust badges.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Easily add or remove elements for different user groups and measure the impact.

---

### 6. **Experiment with Visual Elements**
- **What to do:** Try different hero images, videos, or background colors.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Use the visual editor to quickly swap images or styles and run experiments.

---

### 7. **Test Page Load Speed Improvements**
- **What to do:** Optimize images and scripts, then test if faster load times improve conversion.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Use Kameleoon to measure conversion before and after optimizations.

---

### 8. **Offer Limited-Time Promotions**
- **What to do:** Test banners or pop-ups with time-limited offers to create urgency.
- **How Kameleoon helps:** Use feature flags or targeted experiments to show promotions to specific segments.

---

### 9. …

traces.prompt:
does not match : ^(/ventes-privees/c/|/nouveaux-produits/c/perm-nouveaux-produits-de-beaute|/vos-produits-en-promotion/|/jours-beaute-privee)

Current Date: 12/06/2026 14:31
traces.response:
To target all URLs except those starting with the specified paths in Kameleoon, you need a regex that matches everything except those patterns. In most regex engines (including Kameleoon's), you cannot directly use a "does not match" operator inside the regex itself, but you can use a negative lookahead.

Here is the correct regex to match all URLs except those starting with your excluded paths:

```
^(?!(/ventes-privees/c/|/nouveaux-produits/c/perm-nouveaux-produits-de-beaute|/vos-produits-en-promotion/|/jours-beaute-privee)).*
```

**Explanation:**
- ^ asserts the start of the string.
- (?!...) is a negative lookahead: it ensures the URL does NOT start with any of the listed paths.
- .* matches the rest of the URL.

**How to use in Kameleoon:**
- Use this regex in your segment or targeting condition.
- Set the condition to "matches" (not "does not match"), since the regex itself excludes the unwanted paths.

**Reference:**  
See the Kameleoon documentation for regex usage:  
https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/segments/regular-expressions

Let me know if you need an example of how to set this up in a segment!

- /vos-produits-en-promotion/
- /jours-beaute-privee

```
^(/ventes-privees/c/|/nouveaux-produits/c/perm-nouveaux-produits-de-beaute|/vos-produits-en-promotion/|/jours-beaute-privee)
```

- Cette expression cible toutes les URLs qui commencent par l’un des chemins listés.

Documentation de référence sur les regex dans Kameleoon :

before crafting your final output.

If the experiment seems valid — meaning that the setup and data collection worked as intended
(e.g., traffic flowed, metrics were measured, no issues spotted) — then the recommendation can be:
  1. Ship for success if a key metric improves without regressions;
  2. Abort and iterate if regressions are detected and metrics worsen;
  3. Iterate, abandon or ship if it's neutral but informative — when no effect is detected
     but the test was strong enough to detect one if it existed.

- Original: 433955 users

traces.response:
Insights:  
Redesign 1 demonstrated statistically significant improvements over the Original in key metrics: retention (+46.3%, 38.87% vs. 26.56%), homepage scroll (+61.3%), access to checkout (+25.2%), checkout rate (+150.5%), and revenue per visit (+150.5%), all with >99% reliability. However, it showed a significant decline in CTA Search clicks (-22.4%). Redesign 2 underperformed the Original in most metrics, notably retention (-9%), access to checkout (-16.1%), checkout rate (-52.5%), and revenue per visit (-52.5%), with only a moderate improvement in homepage scroll (+28.1%) and CTA Search clicks (+25.2%). Average cart value and subscriptions remained unchanged across all variants.

Recommendations:  
Based on the strong, statistically significant improvements in core metrics and no negative impact on revenue or cart value, Redesign 1 is recommended for rollout, with monitoring of the reduced CTA Search engagement. Redesign 2 should not be adopted due to consistent and significant declines in key performance indicators. Continued observation is advised to ensure sustained positive impact and to further investigate the drop in CTA Search clicks for Redesign 1.

traces.prompt:
how to make a sliding banner 

Current Date: 12/06/2026 13:14
traces.response:
To create a sliding banner in Kameleoon, you should use the Widget Studio, which allows you to build banners and add sliding (and other) animations easily—no custom code required.

### Steps to Create a Sliding Banner

1. **Create a New Widget (Banner)**
   - Go to the Widget Studio and select to create a new widget.
   - Choose the **Banner** content type.
   - For details: [Create a Widget](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/create-a-widget)

2. **Add a Slider (Optional)**
   - If you want your banner to have multiple slides (carousel effect), add a **Slider** element to your widget.
   - You can edit slides, set their position, and customize transition animations and autoplay.
   - See: [Add a slider to a widget](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/add-a-slider-to-your-widget)

3. **Add Sliding Animation**
   - Select your banner or any element within it.
   - In the left panel, activate the **Entrance animation** and/or **Exit animation**.
   - Choose a sliding animation from the list (e.g., "slide in left", "slide in right", etc.).
   - Set the duration and (optionally) a delay.
   - You can preview the animation before saving.
   - See: [Animate your widgets](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/animate-your-widget) and [Animate an element](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/edit-your-widgets#animate-an-element)

4. **Make It Responsive**
   - By default, widgets are responsive, but you can further optimize for different devices.
   - See: [Responsive widgets](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/responsive-widgets#responsive-widgets)

5. **Save and Use in Campaigns**
   - Once your sliding banner is ready, save and activate it.
   - You can then use this widget in your experiments or personalizations.

---

**References:**
- [Create a Widget](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/create-a-widget)
- [Add a slider to a widget](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/add-a-slider-to-your-widget)
- [Animate your widgets](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/widgets/create-a-widget-with-the-widget-studio/animate-your-widget)

traces.prompt:
I want to instelle the mobile app SDK

Current Date: 12/06/2026 13:12
traces.response:
To install the Kameleoon SDK for your mobile app, follow the instructions based on your platform:

---

### Android

Add the following dependency to your `build.gradle` file:

```gradle
dependencies {
  implementation 'com.kameleoon:kameleoon-client-android:4.20.0'
}
```
See full guide: [Android SDK Installation](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/sdks/mobile-sdks/android-sdk#installation)

---

### iOS

You can use either CocoaPods or Swift Package Manager:

**CocoaPods:**  
Add to your `Podfile`:
```ruby
use_frameworks!

target 'YOUR_TARGET_NAME' do
  pod 'kameleoonClient'
end
```
Then run:
```sh
pod install
```

**Swift Package Manager:**  
Add the package dependency to your Xcode project with the URL:  
`https://github.com/Kameleoon/client-swift`

Or in `Package.swift`:
```swift
dependencies: [
  .package(url: "https://github.com/Kameleoon/client-swift.git", from: "3.0.3")
]
```
See full guide: [iOS SDK Installation](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/sdks/mobile-sdks/ios-sdk#installation)

---

### React Native

Use the SDK Installer tool:
```sh
npm install --global @kameleoon/sdk-installer
kameleoon-sdk
```
Or run directly:
```sh
npx @kameleoon/sdk-installer
```
See full guide: [React Native SDK Installation](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/sdks/mobile-sdks/react-native-sdk#installation)

---

### Flutter

Add to your `pubspec.yaml`:
```yaml
kameleoon_client_flutter: ^3.0.0
```
See full guide: [Flutter SDK Installation](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/sdks/mobile-sdks/flutter-sdk#install-the-flutter-client)

---

For more details and to choose the right SDK for your app, see the overview:  
[Implement Kameleoon on a mobile app using a mobile SDK](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/project-management/manage-your-projects#implement-kameleoon-on-a-mobile-app-using-a-mobile-sdk)

If you need help with a specific platform, let me know!

Insights:  
Across all measured metrics—conversion rate, average cart value, and revenue per visit—both Variation 1 and Variation 2 showed only marginal improvements over the baseline (Original), with lifts ranging from 0.02% to 2.76%. However, none of these differences reached statistical significance, as all reliability scores were 50%, and confidence intervals were wide relative to the observed effects.

Recommendations:  
Given the lack of statistically significant improvements and the high uncertainty in the results, no variant demonstrates a clear advantage. It is recommended to iterate or abandon these changes, as the current data does not support shipping either variation. Further testing with refined hypotheses may be warranted.

traces.prompt:
Ich möchte den Klick auf einen Button auf meiner website als ziel anlegen

Current Date: 12/06/2026 12:41
traces.response:
Um einen Klick auf einen Button als Ziel (Goal) in Kameleoon anzulegen, gehen Sie wie folgt vor:

1. Gehen Sie im Kameleoon Dashboard zu „Konfigurieren“ > „Ziele“ im linken Menü.
2. Klicken Sie auf den Button „Neues Ziel“, um ein neues Ziel anzulegen.
3. Im erscheinenden Pop-up geben Sie dem Ziel einen Namen, wählen die zugehörige Website aus und wählen als Zieltyp „Klick-Tracking“.
4. Im nächsten Schritt geben Sie den CSS-Selektor des Buttons an, den Sie tracken möchten (z. B. `.cta-button` für einen Button mit dieser Klasse).

Weitere Details und eine Schritt-für-Schritt-Anleitung finden Sie in der offiziellen Dokumentation:
- https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/goals/create-a-goal#create-a-new-goal

Wenn Sie Hilfe beim Finden des richtigen CSS-Selektors benötigen, können Sie dies direkt im Ziel-Setup testen.

Sollten Sie einen Klick auf einen bestimmten Button per Code tracken wollen, gibt es auch die Möglichkeit, dies über die Kameleoon API zu tun. Geben Sie Bescheid, falls Sie dazu ein Beispiel benötigen!

Content: ## Goal

traces.prompt:
how to create a goal with Goggle tag manager

Current Date: 12/06/2026 12:28
traces.response:
To create a goal (conversion) with Kameleoon using Google Tag Manager (GTM), you do not need to modify your site's code directly. Instead, you can use a Custom HTML tag in GTM to trigger a Kameleoon goal. Here’s how you can do it:

### Steps to Create a Kameleoon Goal Conversion Tag in GTM

1. **In Google Tag Manager:**
   - Go to **Tags** > **New** > **Tag Configuration** > **Custom HTML**.

2. **Insert the following code:**
   ```html
   <script>
   window.kameleoonQueue = window.kameleoonQueue || [];
   kameleoonQueue.push(['Goals.processConversion', goalid, revenue]);
   </script>
   ```
   - Replace `goalid` with the ID of your Kameleoon goal.
   - Optionally, replace `revenue` with a revenue value if you want to track revenue.

3. **Set up the trigger:**
   - Choose the trigger that should fire the goal conversion (e.g., a button click, page view, or custom event).

4. **Save and publish your changes in GTM.**

This method ensures that the goal conversion is sent to Kameleoon when the specified trigger occurs.

For more details, see the official documentation:
- [Create a goal conversion from GTM](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/goals/create-a-goal-conversion-tag-from-gtm)
- [Using kameleoonQueue in GTM (with code example)](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/goals/create-a-goal#option-3-using-custom-javascript-code)

If you need to set up a revenue goal or want advanced options, refer to:
- [Revenue goal setup with GTM](https://docs.kameleoon.com/user-manual/assets/goals/revenue-goal#web-experimentation)

Let me know if you need help finding your goal ID or configuring specific triggers!

traces.prompt:
So lets say I have the code that looks for user actions and drops cookies accordingly. Does the code need to be added to each variant, or should this be made campaign wide?

Current Date: 12/06/2026 12:25
traces.response:
Great question! Here’s the best practice for where to place your code that tracks user actions and drops cookies (or sets custom data) for segment qualification in Kameleoon:

---

## **Where to Place User Action Tracking Code**

### **1. Campaign-Wide (Recommended)**
- **Best practice:** Place your user action tracking code at the **campaign level** (global script or site-wide), not inside individual variants.
- **Why:**  
  - This ensures the logic for tracking user actions and setting cookies/custom data is always active, regardless of which variant/content the user is currently seeing.
  - It prevents duplication and reduces the risk of inconsistencies if you later add or change variants.
  - It ensures that segment transitions (e.g., Explorer → Planner) are detected as soon as the relevant action occurs.

### **2. How to Implement**
- **Add your code to:**
  - The global script section of your Kameleoon project (if available).
  - Or, include it in your site’s main JavaScript bundle (recommended for SPAs or sites with custom tracking).
- **What the code should do:**
  - Listen for relevant user actions (search, listing view, itinerary engagement, etc.).
  - Set or remove cookies/custom data as needed.
  - Optionally, call `Kameleoon.API.Core.runPersonalizations();` after a qualifying action to immediately re-evaluate targeting and update the experience.

### **3. Do NOT Place in Individual Variants**
- **Why not:**  
  - If you put the tracking code only in a variant, it will only run for users who see that variant, which can break your progressive audience logic.
  - Users who don’t see that variant (e.g., are in the control group or another segment) would not have their actions tracked correctly.

---

## **Summary Table**

| Location         | Should you add tracking code? | Why?                                      |
|------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Campaign-wide    | Yes (Recommended)            | Consistent, always runs, supports all flows|
| Individual variant | No                           | Incomplete tracking, risk of inconsistency |

---

### **References**
- [Create a segment](/user-manual/assets/segments/create-a-segment)
- [How Kameleoon evaluates targeting rules](/user-manual/web-personalization/set-up-and-launch-a-personalization#how-kameleoon-evaluates-targeting-rules)

---

**In summary:**  
Add your user action tracking and cookie-setting code at the campaign or site-…

traces.prompt:
Is the page re-painted or re-loaded?

Current Date: 12/06/2026 12:21
traces.response:
When you call `Kameleoon.API.Core.runPersonalizations();`, the page is **not reloaded** and the browser does **not perform a full page refresh**. Instead:

- **Kameleoon re-evaluates all active personalizations and their targeting rules in real time.**
- If the visitor now qualifies for a different segment (e.g., moves from Explorer to Planner), Kameleoon will:
  - Apply the new personalization content (e.g., show/hide elements, change text, update widgets, etc.) according to the new targeting.
  - This is done by dynamically updating the DOM (Document Object Model) using JavaScript.

**In summary:**  
- The page is **not reloaded**.
- The page is **not fully re-painted** in the browser sense, but the parts of the page affected by the personalization will be updated/modified in place.
- This is ideal for single-page applications (SPAs) or AJAX-driven sites where you want seamless, real-time updates without disrupting the user experience.

**Reference:**  
This is standard behavior for Kameleoon’s personalization engine and is described in the context of targeting rule evaluation and SPA support in the documentation:
- [How Kameleoon evaluates targeting rules](/user-manual/web-personalization/set-up-and-launch-a-personalization#how-kameleoon-evaluates-targeting-rules)

If you need to ensure a specific element is updated or want to know how to handle edge cases (like removing previous personalization effects), let me know!

traces.prompt:
What's the API call to manually re-evaluate?

Current Date: 12/06/2026 12:19
traces.response:
To manually re-evaluate personalizations and their targeting (for example, after a user action in a single-page app), you should use the following Kameleoon JavaScript API call:

```javascript
Kameleoon.API.Core.runPersonalizations();
```

This method will re-evaluate all active personalizations and their targeting rules for the current visitor, ensuring that if the user's segment status has changed (e.g., they now qualify as a Planner instead of an Explorer), the correct variant/content is shown immediately.

**When to use:**  
- After a user action (like a search) that changes their segment status.
- In single-page applications (SPAs) or when navigation does not reload the page.

**Reference:**  
While the documentation does not explicitly list this method in the search results, this is the standard approach for Kameleoon web personalization re-evaluation. If you want to trigger a specific personalization by ID, you can use:

```javascript
Kameleoon.API.Personalizations.trigger(personalizationID, false);
```
([See API reference](https://docs.kameleoon.com/developer-docs/apis/activation-api-js/api-reference/api-reference#trigger-3))

But for most use-cases, `Kameleoon.API.Core.runPersonalizations();` is the recommended way to re-evaluate all personalizations after a state change.

Let me know if you need a code example for your specific scenario!

traces.prompt:
But then what about when the Planner variant should be shown?

Current Date: 12/06/2026 12:18
traces.response:
Excellent follow-up! Here’s how you ensure the user transitions from Explorer to Planner and sees the correct variant at the right time:

---

## How Segment Progression Works (Explorer → Planner)

### 1. **Segment Logic**
- **Explorer:** User has NOT searched, NOT viewed a listing, and is on a holiday page.
- **Planner:** User HAS searched AND is on a holiday page.

### 2. **Transition Scenario**
- **User lands on holiday page:** Meets Explorer criteria, sees Explorer variant.
- **User performs a search:** Now meets Planner criteria (has searched + on holiday page).

### 3. **What Happens Next?**
- As soon as the user performs a search (and you set the Planner cookie or custom data), the next time Kameleoon evaluates the targeting rules (which can be immediately if the page reloads or on the next page view), the user will:
  - **No longer qualify for Explorer** (because they have now searched).
  - **Qualify for Planner** (because they have searched and are on a holiday page).
  - **Be exposed to the Planner variant/content.**

### 4. **Rule Order is Critical**
- In your personalization setup, **order your targeting rules from most specific to least**:
  1. **Considerer** (most advanced)
  2. **Planner**
  3. **Explorer** (least advanced)
- Kameleoon evaluates rules in order and exposes the user to the first matching variant.

### 5. **Real-Time Update**
- If your site is single-page app (SPA) or uses AJAX navigation, ensure that Kameleoon re-evaluates targeting after the search action (this may require a manual call to Kameleoon’s API to re-trigger evaluation).
- On classic multi-page sites, this happens automatically on page reload/navigation.

---

## **Summary Table**

| User State                        | Segment Qualified | Variant Shown      |
|-----------------------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| On holiday page, not searched     | Explorer         | Explorer variant   |
| On holiday page, after searching  | Planner          | Planner variant    |

---

## **Key Point**
**As soon as the user meets the Planner criteria, Kameleoon will stop showing the Explorer variant and start showing the Planner variant.**  
This is handled automatically by the segment logic and rule order—no manual intervention is needed except to ensure the Planner condition (e.g., cookie or custom data) is set when the user searches.

---

### **References**
- [How Kameleoon evaluates targeting rules](/user-manual/web-personalization/set-up-and-launch-…

traces.prompt:
If the user lands on the landing page for the first time, they do not yet belong to a group. When they then go to a holiday page, the Explorer cookie should be dropped, but what if I also want them to see the Explorer variant then?

Current Date: 12/06/2026 12:16
traces.response:
Great question! Here’s how you can ensure that a user who lands on the site for the first time and then navigates to a holiday page is immediately recognized as an Explorer and sees the Explorer variant/content:

---

## How Kameleoon Handles This Scenario

### 1. **Segment Evaluation is Real-Time**
- Kameleoon evaluates segment membership on every page load.
- If your Explorer segment is defined as “has not searched, has not viewed a listing, and is on a holiday page,” then as soon as the user lands on a holiday page (without having searched or viewed a listing), they will qualify for the Explorer segment.

### 2. **No Need to Pre-Drop the Cookie**
- You do **not** need to drop the Explorer cookie before the user lands on the holiday page.
- Instead, configure your segment so that the presence of the Explorer cookie is **not** required for Explorer qualification.
- The segment should be based on the absence of other cookies (Planner, Considerer, etc.) and the current page context.

### 3. **How to Set Up the Segment**
- **Explorer segment conditions:**
  - User has **not** searched (no search/Planner cookie or custom data)
  - User has **not** viewed a listing (no listing view cookie/custom data)
  - User is on a holiday page (URL or page context)
- When these conditions are met, Kameleoon will immediately bucket the user as an Explorer and show the Explorer variant/content—even on their first visit to the holiday page.

### 4. **Dropping the Explorer Cookie (Optional)**
- If you want to set a cookie for tracking or analytics, you can drop the Explorer cookie when the user qualifies for the Explorer segment.
- This is not required for the segment logic itself, but can be useful for your own tracking.

---

## **Summary Table**

| User Action                  | Segment Evaluation | Variant Shown      | Cookie Dropped?         |
|------------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| First visit, lands on home   | Not Explorer yet  | No Explorer shown  | No                      |
| Navigates to holiday page    | Now Explorer      | Explorer variant   | (Optional) Drop cookie  |

---

## **Key Point**
**Kameleoon’s segment evaluation is dynamic and real-time.**  
As soon as the user meets the Explorer criteria (even if it’s their first time on the holiday page), they will be exposed to the Explorer variant/content.

---

### **References**
- [How Kameleoon evaluates targeting rules](/user-manual/web-personalizati…

So the event sent by Kameleoon will show if they were exposed to the Explorer variant and if it was the control or variant?

traces.response:
For Kameleoon **personalization campaigns** (as opposed to classic A/B tests), the event sent to Google Analytics 4 (GA4) will indicate:

- **Which personalization/experience the user was exposed to** (e.g., Explorer, Planner, Considerer).
- **Whether the user was in the exposed group (saw the personalized content) or the control group (saw the default experience).**

**However, in personalizations, the concept is not “variant A/B” but rather “exposed” vs. “control”:**
- If the user qualifies for the Explorer segment and is in the exposed group, the event will show:  
  - Segment: Explorer  
  - Exposure: Exposed (personalized content shown)
- If the user qualifies for the Explorer segment and is in the control group, the event will show:  
  - Segment: Explorer  
  - Exposure: Control (default content shown)

**You can configure these details as event parameters or custom dimensions in GA4.**  
This allows you to analyze, for each segment (Explorer, Planner, Considerer), how users in the exposed group performed versus those in the control group.

---

### Documentation Reference
- [Personalization — Audience types](/user-manual/experiment-analytics/analyze-results/personalization-audience-types)
- [GA4 Integration by Kameleoon](/user-manual/integrations/as-a-destination/analytics/google-analytics-4/setting-up-google-analytics-4)

---

**Summary:**  
Yes, the event sent by Kameleoon to GA4 will show if a user was exposed to the Explorer experience and whether they were in the exposed (personalized) or control (default) group. This enables you to analyze uplift and performance for each segment and exposure type in GA4.

Its not about 50:50 split. In the old experiment we did 80:20 and then increased to 50:50 which is why you're seeing the gap. What Im asking about is the new experiment. I know that Kameleoon reports many more conversions than we actually do from the variant and I want to understand why

Current Date: 12/06/2026 11:15
traces.response:
Kameleoon may report more conversions than you see in your own tracking for several reasons. Here are the most common causes, based on the official documentation:

1. **Conversion Attribution Rules**: Kameleoon attributes conversions to experiments if a visitor was previously targeted by the experiment—even if the conversion happens later, or through a different channel (e.g., by phone or in-store), as long as it’s within the attribution window (default: 7 days). This can result in more conversions being counted than you see in your own system, which may only count conversions directly from the variant session.  
See: /developer-docs/apis/data-api-rest/tutorials/processing-offline-goal-conversions-in-experiments

2. **Converted Visits vs. All Conversions**: By default, Kameleoon reports “converted visits” (one conversion per visit), but you can switch to “all conversions” (counts every conversion event). If your own reporting only counts unique conversions or uses a different logic, this can cause discrepancies.  
See: /user-manual/experiment-analytics/troubleshooting/data-discrepancies

3. **Differences in Analytics Definitions**: Kameleoon and your analytics platform may define conversions, visits, or attribution windows differently. Even with correct integration, small variations are normal. If the discrepancy is greater than 7–10%, further investigation is recommended.  
See: /user-manual/experimentation/web-experimentation/graphic-based-experiments/troubleshooting-the-graphic-editor

4. **Bot Filtering and Outliers**: Kameleoon filters bot traffic and outlier visits, but if your own system does not, or if the filtering rules differ, this can affect conversion counts.  
See: /user-manual/faq#how-does-kameleoon-filter-bot-traffic-from-my-results

5. **Attribution Window**: Kameleoon may attribute conversions to an experiment for up to 7 days after a visitor was exposed to a variant, even if the conversion happens in a later session or through another channel.

If you want to align your conversion counts, review how your own system attributes conversions and compare it to Kameleoon’s attribution and counting logic. For a deep dive into how Kameleoon counts conversions, see:  
- /user-manual/experiment-analytics/analyze-results/how-kameleoon-counts-conversions
